Problem: Multiple notes guards can apply to a single run, and which body mint uses depends on strict precedence. mint must resolve in order: (1) first-release fixed body, (2) degenerate stub, (3) --no-ai fallback, (4) normal AI path. Getting it wrong means e.g. --no-ai triggering the AI, or a first-release run routed through on_notes_failure. This resolver is the single decision point selecting which path produces the body.

Solution: A precedence resolver that, given run state (prior tag present?, --no-ai?, post-exclusion diff) and config, selects exactly one notes path in fixed order: first-release > degenerate > --no-ai > normal AI. Routes each run to the right body provider (1-9, 2-8, 2-9, 2-6) and ensures on_notes_failure (2-7) governs only the normal AI path.

Outcome: first-release (no prior tag) always uses 'Initial release.' and wins over --no-ai and a degenerate diff — no AI, no on_notes_failure. Degenerate wins over --no-ai (stub, no AI). --no-ai wins over normal AI (fallback, no AI, never aborts). Only when none of the first three apply does the normal AI path run, failures routed through on_notes_failure.

Do:
- internal/notes: SelectBody(ctx, state, cfg) (body string, path Kind, err error) resolving precedence strictly:
  1. First release (no prior tag) -> fixed body 'Initial release.' (1-9), no AI. Wins over everything below — no diff base, so --no-ai, on_notes_failure, and the degenerate check do not apply.
  2. Degenerate diff (empty / all-excluded post-exclusion diff — 2-8) -> stub, no AI.
  3. --no-ai (deliberate skip — 2-9) -> fallback body, never aborts.
  4. Normal AI path (2-6) -> generate + validate; on failure route to on_notes_failure (2-7).
- Evaluate first-release BEFORE consulting --no-ai or degenerate; degenerate BEFORE --no-ai. For first-release the degenerate check must not even run (no diff base).
- Ensure on_notes_failure consulted ONLY in branch 4. Branches 1-3 never call the AI / never route through on_notes_failure.
- Return which path was taken (a Kind) so the review gate (2-14) knows whether to offer r (only on normal AI path) and the wiring (2-16) can report it.
- Single decision point invoked by end-to-end wiring (2-16); composes existing body providers, does not reimplement them.

Acceptance Criteria:
- First-release (no prior tag) selects 'Initial release.' and wins over --no-ai and a degenerate diff.
- First-release never runs the degenerate check or on_notes_failure (no diff base).
- A degenerate diff wins over --no-ai (stub, no AI).
- --no-ai wins over the normal AI path (fallback, no AI, never aborts).
- The normal AI path runs only when none of the first three apply.
- on_notes_failure governs only the normal AI path (branches 1-3 never reach it).
- Resolver reports which path was taken (for gate r-omission and reporting).

Tests: first-release wins over --no-ai and a degenerate diff; first-release does not run the degenerate check or on_notes_failure; a degenerate diff wins over --no-ai; --no-ai wins over the normal AI path; the normal AI path runs only when no earlier guard applies; on_notes_failure governs only the normal AI path; resolver reports the selected path kind.

Edge Cases: first-release wins over --no-ai and degenerate; degenerate wins over --no-ai; on_notes_failure only governs the normal path.

Context: Notes-path precedence — resolves in order: 1 first release (no prior tag) -> fixed body 'Initial release.' no AI, wins over everything below (no diff base, so --no-ai, on_notes_failure, degenerate check don't apply); 2 degenerate diff (empty/all-excluded post-diff_exclude) -> stub entry no AI; 3 --no-ai (deliberate skip) -> fallback body (commit-subject list) never aborts; 4 normal AI path -> generate+validate, failures route to on_notes_failure. on_notes_failure governs only the normal AI path; steps 1-3 never call the AI.

Spec Reference: specification.md — Stage 4 Notes-path precedence.

title: Single-body distribution to all sinks
description:
Problem: The single notes body feeds three surfaces — the annotated tag, CHANGELOG.md, and the provider release — but mint WRITES all three and READS only one (the tag annotation). The body must be used WHOLE for every sink with no parsing/per-sink reassembly, and optional sinks must honour toggles: changelog=false skips the CHANGELOG (tag still carries the full body), publish=false skips the provider.

Solution: A distribution step taking the selected body (whatever path produced it — 2-10) and threading it whole into existing sinks: annotated tag (Phase 1 tag writer — sole read source), CHANGELOG projection (Phase 1 writer, skipped when changelog=false), provider release (Phase 1 Publisher.CreateRelease, skipped when publish=false). Identical body to every active sink.

Solution note: annotated tag (1-10), changelog writer (1-9), GitHub Publisher (1-8) already exist from Phase 1. This task GENERALISES the body from the fixed 'Initial release.' to any Phase 2 body and enforces toggles — does not rebuild sinks.

Outcome: same body string written verbatim to the annotated tag (subject {commit_prefix} Release {tag} + full body), the CHANGELOG ## [x.y.z] - date section, and the provider release. changelog=false skips CHANGELOG but tag still carries full body. publish=false creates no provider release. Body identical across active sinks — no parsing/splitting/reassembly.

Do:
- In release orchestration (extending Phase 1 record/tag/publish wiring), thread the selected body (from 2-10) into the three sinks instead of the hardcoded first-release body:
  - Annotated tag (1-10): subject {commit_prefix} Release {tag} + full body. The single source mint ever reads.
  - CHANGELOG.md (1-9 writer): full body under ## [x.y.z] - YYYY-MM-DD header. Skip entirely when changelog=false.
  - Provider release (1-8 CreateRelease): full body. Skip when publish=false.
- Read changelog (default true) and publish (default true) toggles from [release] config (Phase 1 loads publish; add changelog toggle read here default true). Full schema validation Phase 6.
- Use the body whole for every sink — no parsing/splitting/label extraction/per-sink reassembly. Assert identical string reaches each active sink.
- changelog=false: nothing durable lost — tag still holds the full body (floor / source of truth).
- Provider auto-detection and unknown-provider/no-driver downgrade are Phase 4 — assume the Phase 1 GitHub Publisher when publish=true.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Same body string written to the annotated tag, the CHANGELOG section, and the provider release.
- Body used whole — no parsing/splitting/reassembly anywhere.
- changelog=false skips the CHANGELOG projection; tag still carries the full body.
- publish=false skips the provider release.
- Annotated tag always written and always carries the body (mandatory floor / sole read source).
- Toggles read from config with defaults (changelog/publish default true).

Tests: identical body written to tag, changelog, and provider release; body used whole with no parsing or per-sink reassembly; changelog=false skips the CHANGELOG but the tag still carries the body; publish=false skips the provider release; annotated tag always written with the full body.

Edge Cases: changelog=false -> skip CHANGELOG (tag still carries body); publish=false -> skip provider; identical body across sinks.

Context: Body Distribution — single notes body feeds three surfaces; mint writes all three but reads only one (tag annotation). Tag annotation = subject {commit_prefix} Release {tag} + full notes body, the single source mint ever reads. CHANGELOG.md = write-only projection of the full body. Provider release = write-only projection. Optionality stack: annotated tag mandatory always carries body (floor/source of truth); provider release optional (publish default true); CHANGELOG.md optional (changelog default true). changelog=false: nothing durable lost — tag still holds the full notes. Output: body whole for every sink, no parsing/splitting/per-sink reassembly. Provider auto-detection/downgrade Phase 4.

Spec Reference: specification.md — Body Distribution: Tag vs Changelog vs Provider Release.

title: Interactive review gate semantics (y/n/e)
description:
Problem: The biggest live pain with the legacy script is release notes go out unseen. Notes generate before any mutation, so there is a zero-risk window to review them. mint must drive the review gate's SEMANTICS — y accept (bare Enter), n abort, e edit verbatim — through the Phase 1 Presenter decision seam, before any mutation, with -y auto-accepting INSIDE the Prompt call (the engine still requests the decision). (r is 2-14; abort auto-unwind is 2-15; editor resolution is 2-13.)

Solution: A review-gate driver that, after the plan + notes are shown via the Presenter, requests the user's choice through the Phase 1 Presenter decision seam and maps the three core choices: y (accept — default, bare Enter) proceeds to Record; n (abort) stops the run pre-mutation; e (edit) replaces the body with user-edited text used VERBATIM (no re-validate). Under -y the engine STILL calls Prompt; the presenter skips rendering/reading internally, emits the rendered auto-accept echo, and returns the gate default — notes used as generated. Gate runs BEFORE any mutation.

Solution note: owns y/n/e semantics and the -y behaviour, driving the Phase 1 Presenter decision seam. Gate RENDERING (default-yes Continue? prompt, menu layout, line-read input, the -y auto-accept echo) is the CLI Presentation spec — do NOT implement rendering. r (2-14), editor resolution (2-13), and full abort auto-unwind (2-15) are separate; here n surfaces the abort intent.

Outcome: After plan + notes shown, gate requests a choice via the Presenter seam. Bare Enter / y accepts and proceeds to Record. n aborts before any mutation (no tag/commit). e swaps in the user-edited body, used verbatim with no re-validation, then proceeds. -y auto-accepts via Prompt's internal skip: Prompt still fires, the presenter emits the auto-accept echo and returns the gate default, and the run proceeds with notes as generated. Gate always runs before the first mutation.

Do:
- In release orchestration, after showing plan + the generated/selected body via the Presenter (1-7), invoke the decision seam on the Presenter to obtain the review choice. Engine owns semantics; Presenter owns rendering (cross-spec boundary documented on the Phase 1 interface).
- Map the three core choices:
  - y accept (default; bare Enter): proceed to Record -> tag -> push (Phase 1 spine).
  - n abort: stop before any mutation. Surface the abort; full auto-unwind (when a pre_tag hook-artifact commit exists) is 2-15 — in Phase 2's hook-free forward path, abort = surface and stop with nothing mutated. Wire n to the abort path here.
  - e edit: obtain the edited text (editor launch/resolution is 2-13) and use it VERBATIM — no re-parse, no validation. Human edit trusted; structural validation only for untrusted AI output. Replace the body, then proceed.
- -y / --yes: the engine STILL CALLS Prompt(gate) — it must NOT branch around the call. The skip happens INSIDE Prompt: the presenter skips rendering/reading internally, emits the rendered auto-accept echo ('notes: accepted (-y)' plain / '✓ notes  accepted (-y)' pretty — presenter-rendered, never engine-printed), and returns the gate default. The run proceeds on the returned default with notes as generated.
- Ensure the gate is positioned BEFORE any mutation (before Record). Notes generate before the point of no return, so review is zero-risk.
- Test via RecordingPresenter scripting each choice: accept proceeds, abort stops pre-mutation, edit uses verbatim text; under -y assert Prompt fired exactly once (recorder records KindPrompt) and the run proceeded on the returned default.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Bare Enter / y accepts and proceeds to Record.
- n aborts before any mutation (no tag, no commit).
- e replaces the body with user-edited text used VERBATIM — no re-parse, no validation — then proceeds.
- Under -y / --yes the engine still calls Prompt exactly once; the presenter auto-accepts internally (rendered echo, returns the gate default) and the run proceeds with notes as generated — the engine never branches around the Prompt call.
- The gate runs before any mutation (before Record).
- Gate drives the Phase 1 Presenter decision seam; rendering (including the -y auto-accept echo) not implemented here.

Tests: a bare Enter accepts and proceeds to Record; y accepts and proceeds; n aborts before any mutation; e uses the saved text verbatim with no re-validation; under -y Prompt fires exactly once (recorder records KindPrompt) and the run proceeds on the returned default with notes as generated — the auto-accept echo is presenter-rendered, not engine-printed; the gate runs before any mutation.

Edge Cases: bare Enter -> accept; e saved text verbatim (no re-validate); -y auto-accepts inside Prompt (engine still calls it, exactly once); gate before any mutation.

Context: Interactive review — notes generated at Stage 4 before any mutation / the point of no return, a zero-risk window. Gate (Continue?): [y] accept (default Enter) [n] abort [e] edit [r] regenerate with context. y accept (bare Enter) -> proceed to Record -> tag -> push. n abort -> full auto-unwind. e edit -> opens the notes in the user's editor for real manual editing; saved text used verbatim, no re-parse/validation; human edit trusted, structural validation only for untrusted AI output. Non-interactive: -y / --yes auto-accepts (uses notes as generated) — implemented INSIDE Prompt: the presenter skips rendering/reading, emits the rendered auto-accept echo, and returns the gate default; the engine always calls Prompt. Exact gate rendering owned by the CLI Presentation specification; this section owns the four semantic choices and effects. r is 2-14; editor resolution 2-13; abort auto-unwind 2-15.

Spec Reference: specification.md — Interactive Confirmation & Notes Review.

title: Editor resolution for e (edit choice)
description:
Problem: The e edit choice opens the notes in the user's editor for real manual editing. mint must resolve which editor to launch — $VISUAL, then $EDITOR, then a sensible default (vi) — and, critically, if no editor can be launched, report the problem and RETURN TO THE GATE rather than crashing. Without robust resolution, e is fragile and can strand or crash a release at the review step.

Solution: An editor-resolution + launch helper that resolves via $VISUAL -> $EDITOR -> vi, writes the current body to a temp file, launches the resolved editor on it, returns the saved text. The hand-off is bracketed with the presenter's spinner controls so nothing animates while the editor owns the terminal. If no editor can be launched, report the problem and signal return-to-gate so the caller re-presents the gate instead of crashing.

Solution note: owns editor resolution and launch for the e choice; the gate semantics that invoke it (and use the result verbatim) are 2-12. The launched editor runs through the CommandRunner so it can be faked in tests.

Outcome: With $VISUAL set, that editor launches; else $EDITOR; else vi. Current body presented for editing, saved text returned (used verbatim by 2-12). The spinner is suspended for the duration of the editor session and resumed after. If none of the resolved editors can launch, mint reports the problem and returns to the gate (no crash, nothing mutated).

Do:
- ResolveEditor() string: return $VISUAL if set and non-empty; else $EDITOR if set and non-empty; else default vi.
- Edit launch helper: write current body to a temp file, launch the resolved editor on it via CommandRunner (interactive — editor takes over the terminal), read the saved file back as the new body. Saved text returned to the caller (2-12) and used VERBATIM (no validation here).
- Wrap the editor hand-off with the presenter's spinner controls: call presenter.SuspendSpinner() before launching the editor and presenter.ResumeSpinner() after it returns (both are safe no-ops in plain mode and when nothing is animating) — the editor takes over the terminal, so nothing may animate while it runs.
- No launchable editor: if launching the resolved editor fails (command-not-found for the resolved binary — reuse the 1-1 command-not-found signal), do NOT crash: report via the Presenter and signal the caller to return to the gate (re-present choices). Nothing mutated.
- Clean up the temp file after reading it back.
- Test editor resolution by setting/unsetting $VISUAL/$EDITOR and asserting which binary the FakeRunner was asked to launch; test the no-launchable-editor path returns the back-to-gate signal without crashing; assert the spinner suspend/resume bracketing via the recorder.

Acceptance Criteria:
- $VISUAL (set) is preferred for the editor.
- With $VISUAL unset and $EDITOR set, $EDITOR is used.
- With neither set, vi is the default.
- Current body written to a temp file, editor launched on it, saved text returned.
- presenter.SuspendSpinner() is called before the editor launches and presenter.ResumeSpinner() after it returns (safe no-ops in plain mode and when nothing is animating).
- If no resolved editor can be launched, mint reports the problem and returns to the gate (no crash, no mutation).
- Temp file cleaned up.
- Editor launch goes through CommandRunner/FakeRunner.

Tests: prefers $VISUAL when set; uses $EDITOR when only $EDITOR set; falls back to vi when neither set; returns the saved text from the temp file; recorder Kinds() show KindSuspendSpinner before and KindResumeSpinner after the editor invocation; no launchable editor reports the problem and returns to the gate without crashing.

Edge Cases: $VISUAL set; only $EDITOR set; neither -> vi; no launchable editor -> report and return to gate; spinner suspended/resumed around the editor session.

Context: Interactive review — e edit opens the notes in the user's editor for real manual editing; saved text used verbatim, no re-parse/validation. Editor resolution: $VISUAL then $EDITOR, falling back to a sensible default (vi); if no editor can be launched, mint reports the problem and returns to the gate rather than crashing. The presenter owns the terminal animation, so the engine brackets the hand-off with SuspendSpinner/ResumeSpinner. The verbatim use of the result is 2-12.

Spec Reference: specification.md — Interactive Confirmation & Notes Review (e edit).

title: r regenerate-with-context (loop) & no-AI gate variant
description:
Problem: The r choice is the nudge-it-just-this-once affordance: mint asks for a one-time context line, appends it to the prompt, re-runs the AI, shows the result again, looping until happy — without permanently editing [release].context. But on no-AI notes paths (first-release fixed body, degenerate stub, --no-ai fallback) there is no AI to nudge, so r must be OMITTED from the gate.

Solution: Extend the review gate (2-12) with the r choice on the normal AI path: read a one-time context line via presenter.AskLine, append it to the prompt, re-run the normal AI path (2-6), re-show the result, loop (re-presenting the gate) until y/n/e. On no-AI paths (identified by the precedence Kind from 2-10) the gate offers only y/n/e — r omitted. The one-time context line is NOT persisted to config.

Solution note: in-gate one-time-context nudge ONLY — not the standalone regenerate/--all command (Phase 5). The context line lives for this run's loop only and never touches [release].context.

Outcome: On the normal AI path, r asks for a context line via AskLine, appends it to the prompt, regenerates via the AI, re-shows the gate; repeated r loops keep nudging (each appends its line for that regeneration). The one-time line never written to [release].context. On first-release, degenerate, and --no-ai paths, gate omits r (only y/n/e). Choosing y/n/e exits the loop.

Do:
- Extend the gate driver (2-12) with the r regenerate choice, available ONLY when the precedence Kind (2-10) is the normal AI path:
  - Read the one-time context line via presenter.AskLine(prompt) (string, error) — one free-text line through the presenter's persistent input reader. The engine must NEVER read stdin directly; the presenter owns the buffered reader. An empty string is legal and means no context (regenerate with no extra nudge). On errors.Is(err, ErrInputClosed) -> abort fail-loud (engine-owned non-zero exit). Note -y never reaches this path: r is only reachable interactively.
  - Append it to the prompt for this regeneration (compose alongside default/overridden prompt + Change Map + diff — 2-5/2-6), re-run the normal AI path (2-6), re-show the result.
  - Loop: re-present the gate; the user may r again (another nudge), or settle on y/n/e. Each r regenerates and re-shows.
- The one-time context line is NOT persisted to [release].context (transient nudge for this run only). Assert it never mutates config.
- No-AI gate variant: when the Kind is first-release / degenerate / --no-ai, the gate offers only y/n/e — r omitted (no AI to nudge; offering it under --no-ai would contradict the flag). y/n body-agnostic and e edits verbatim regardless of source, so only r is affected. To regenerate with the AI on a --no-ai run, the user re-runs without --no-ai (documented) — r never silently overrides the flag.
- Test multiple r loops, omission of r on each no-AI path, and that the context line does not reach config; drive RecordingPresenter + FakeRunner (scripting AskLine answers via the recorder's NextLines / AskLineResult, and successive AI bodies per regeneration).

Acceptance Criteria:
- On the normal AI path, r reads a one-time context line via presenter.AskLine, appends it to the prompt, regenerates via the AI, re-shows the gate.
- The engine never reads stdin directly — the context line comes through the presenter's AskLine input seam.
- An empty AskLine answer is legal: regenerate with no extra context.
- errors.Is ErrInputClosed from AskLine aborts fail-loud.
- Multiple r loops supported; each regenerates and re-shows.
- The one-time context line not persisted to [release].context.
- On first-release, degenerate, and --no-ai paths, the gate omits r (only y/n/e).
- r never overrides --no-ai (the user re-runs without the flag to use the AI).
- Choosing y/n/e exits the regenerate loop.
- AI regeneration goes through the existing normal AI path (2-6) and CommandRunner/FakeRunner.

Tests: r reads a one-time context line via AskLine (scripted with NextLines / AskLineResult), appends it, and regenerates via the AI; an empty AskLine answer regenerates with no extra context; ErrInputClosed from AskLine aborts fail-loud; multiple r loops each regenerate and re-show the gate; the one-time context line not persisted to config; r omitted on the first-release path; r omitted on the degenerate path; r omitted on the --no-ai path; choosing y/n/e exits the regenerate loop.

Edge Cases: r omitted on first-release/degenerate/--no-ai paths; multiple r loops; context line not persisted to config; empty context line legal (no context); ErrInputClosed aborts; -y never reaches r (interactive-only path).

Context: Interactive review — r regenerate with context: mint asks for a one-time context line, appends it to the prompt, re-runs the AI, shows the result again (loops until happy); nudge-it-just-this-once without permanently editing [release].context. The line is read through the presenter's persistent input reader via AskLine — the engine never touches stdin. On the no-AI notes paths (first-release fixed body, degenerate stub, --no-ai fallback) the gate offers only y/n/e: the AI-dependent r regenerate omitted, no AI invocation to nudge (offering it under --no-ai would contradict the flag); to regenerate with the AI on a --no-ai run, the user re-runs without --no-ai rather than r silently overriding it; y/n body-agnostic, e edits verbatim regardless of source, so only r affected. Standalone regenerate/--all command is Phase 5 — this is the in-gate nudge only.

Spec Reference: specification.md — Interactive Confirmation & Notes Review (r regenerate, no-AI variant).

title: Abort auto-unwind from the gate (n)
description:
Problem: Answering n at the review gate must roll back EVERYTHING mint made this run, returning the repo to the exact clean starting state — identical to the pre-push failure path. A user-abort and a pre-push git failure are treated identically: nothing mint did this run survives unless the release completes. Without wiring n to the unwind, an abort could leave a stray release commit behind.

Solution: Wire the gate's n choice to the auto-unwind: reset any commit(s) mint created this run (in Phase 2's hook-free forward path this is the release-bookkeeping commit, if Record has run) and return to the exact clean starting state, reusing the Phase 1 spine's reset. The same path serves a user-abort and a pre-push failure. Report the result via the as-built Unwound(Unwind{Summary}) event.

Solution note: Phase 2 keeps n to the gate-abort behaviour reusing the Phase 1 spine reset. The hardened, surgical, lock-resilient auto-unwind (precise N-commits-+-tag deletion, --autostash restore ordering) is PHASE 4 — do not build that here; note it as deferred. In Phase 2's forward path the gate runs BEFORE any mutation in the common case, so abort frequently has nothing to undo; the wiring must still correctly reset if Record-stage commits exist (and will reset the hook-artifact commit too once hooks land in Phase 3).

Outcome: Answering n returns the repo to the exact clean state it started from — no release commit, no tag survives. Abort path identical to the pre-push failure path. Because the gate sits before any mutation in Phase 2's forward path, abort typically has nothing to undo; if any mint commit exists at abort time, it is reset. The abort is reported via Unwound with an engine-authored summary, and no success end-of-run line follows.

Do:
- Wire the gate's n choice (from 2-12) to the auto-unwind / clean-reset path:
  - Reset any commit(s) mint created this run back to the recorded clean starting point (the HEAD mint captured before mutating). Reuse the Phase 1 spine reset mechanism.
  - No tag is created until after the gate in the spine, so in the common case there is no tag to delete at gate-abort; if a tag were created this run, it would be removed (surgical version is Phase 4).
- Treat a user-abort and a pre-push git failure identically — route both to the same clean-reset path (one mental model).
- Capture the clean starting state (e.g. starting HEAD) at run start so the unwind target is unambiguous.
- Report what was undone via Unwound(Unwind{Summary}): the ENGINE authors the entire Summary string, INCLUDING the 'repo clean' tail — the presenter renders the Summary verbatim, prefixing only the unwound label/glyph; it never synthesises the tail. E.g. a summary of what was reset ending in the repo-clean statement; if nothing was mutated, the Summary says so. Unwound also suppresses the success end-of-run line automatically; the engine still owns the exit code.
- Do NOT implement Phase 4 hardening: surgical precise-count unwind, lock-resilient git, --autostash stash-restore ordering. Keep to the gate-abort reset using the Phase 1 spine; note hardening deferred to Phase 4.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Answering n returns the repo to the exact clean starting state.
- No release commit and no tag survive an abort.
- Abort path identical to the pre-push failure path (same reset).
- Abort reporting goes through Unwound(Unwind{Summary}) with the engine-authored Summary including the repo-clean tail (the presenter renders it verbatim and never synthesises the tail).
- If the gate is reached before any mutation, the Unwound Summary reports nothing to undo (nothing changed).
- If a mint commit exists at abort time, it is reset to the clean starting point.
- No success end-of-run line follows an unwound run (Unwound suppresses it); the engine still owns the exit code.
- Phase 4 surgical/lock-resilient/autostash hardening NOT implemented (deferred).
- All git operations via CommandRunner/FakeRunner.

Tests: answering n returns the repo to the clean starting state; no commit or tag survives an abort; the abort path matches the pre-push failure path; abort before any mutation emits an Unwound Summary reporting nothing to undo; abort after a Record commit resets that commit; the Unwound event carries the engine-authored Summary including the repo-clean tail (recorder asserts the event); no success end-of-run line follows an unwound run.

Edge Cases: unwind back to clean state; identical to pre-push failure path; no tag/commit survives; engine authors the full Unwound Summary incl. the repo-clean tail.

Context: Interactive review — n abort -> full auto-unwind: identical to the pre-push failure path; mint rolls back everything it made this run, including any pre_tag hook-artifact commit, returning to the exact clean starting state; the hook re-runs next time (idempotent build); a user-abort and a pre-push git failure treated identically. Failure model (before the push): everything mint did is local-only; mint auto-unwinds its own mutations — deletes the tag it made, resets the release commit(s) — returning to the exact clean starting state; mint knows precisely what it created (N commits + 1 tag), so the unwind is surgical; not configurable (YAGNI). Invariants: nothing mint did this run survives unless the release completes. Reporting goes through the as-built Unwound(Unwind{Summary}) event: the engine authors the whole Summary (incl. the repo-clean tail), the presenter renders it verbatim behind the unwound label/glyph and suppresses the success end-of-run line. Surgical/lock-resilient/autostash-restore hardening is Phase 4; the hook-artifact commit lands in Phase 3 — Phase 2's n reuses the Phase 1 spine reset for the gate-abort behaviour.

Spec Reference: specification.md — Interactive Confirmation & Notes Review (n abort); Stages 6-7 Failure model; Release Lifecycle Invariants.

title: End-to-end prior-tag release wiring
description:
Problem: The Phase 2 pieces — context assembly, max_diff_lines guard, Change Map, default prompt, transport, the four notes paths + precedence, single-body distribution, and the y/n/e/r review gate — exist independently, but nothing threads them into a runnable prior-tag mint release that takes a repo WITH a prior tag to a published release whose AI-generated body flows to all three sinks and is gated on review. This vertical seam proves the engine end-to-end and completes the Presenter cross-spec seam by exercising all four review choices.

Solution: Extend the Phase 1 release orchestrator so a release WITH a prior tag runs the full Phase 2 notes flow: select body via precedence (2-10) -> show plan + notes -> run the y/n/e/r gate (2-12/2-13/2-14; the engine ALWAYS calls Prompt — under -y the skip happens inside Prompt) -> on accept, distribute the single body whole to the tag, CHANGELOG, and provider (2-11) through the Phase 1 Record -> tag -> push -> publish spine; on abort, auto-unwind to clean (2-15).

Outcome: In a repo with a prior tag, mint release generates the notes body via the appropriate notes path, shows it at the gate, and — on accept (or the -y auto-accept default) — records it, tags, atomic-pushes, and publishes, with the IDENTICAL body in all three sinks. Accept proceeds to Record; abort (n) leaves the repo clean. The --no-ai, normal-AI, and degenerate paths all run end-to-end; the PONR asymmetry from Phase 1 (warn-only post-push) is preserved.

Do:
- Extend the Phase 1 orchestrator (cmd/mint / release orchestration) so the notes stage for a prior-tag release uses the Phase 2 precedence resolver (2-10) instead of the hardcoded first-release body. First-release (no prior tag) continues to use 'Initial release.' via the same resolver (precedence step 1) — Phase 1's path is now one branch of the unified selector.
- Spine order for a prior-tag release:
  1. Version -> preflight (Phase 1) -> determine last_tag (current latest, 1-3).
  2. Notes: SelectBody (2-10) -> body + path Kind (handles first-release / degenerate / --no-ai / normal AI, with on_notes_failure routing on the normal path).
  3. Show plan + notes via Presenter; the engine ALWAYS calls Prompt for the review gate (2-12/2-13/2-14) — under -y the skip happens INSIDE Prompt (rendered auto-accept echo, returns the gate default). r available only on the normal AI path.
  4. On accept (incl. the -y auto-accepted default): Record (changelog + bookkeeping commit) -> gh gate (if publishing, before tag) -> annotated tag + git push --atomic -> publish — distributing the single body whole to all sinks (2-11).
  5. On abort (n): auto-unwind to clean (2-15).
- Event protocol — the run emits the as-built Presenter events in order: RunStarted -> stages (StageStarted/StageSucceeded carrying engine-set Blocking and engine-measured Elapsed) -> ShowPlan -> ShowNotes -> Prompt(NotesReviewGate()) -> ... -> RunFinished(RunResult{Project,Version,URL,Leaf,Verb,Summary}) on success; the gate-n abort path ends in Unwound instead of RunFinished.
- Preserve the PONR asymmetry: pre-push failures abort cleanly; a publish failure after a successful push warns only (Phase 1 behaviour, unchanged).
- Wire --no-ai (selects the fallback path via precedence) into the release command flags' behaviour for this end-to-end path. (-d/--dry-run, --set-version, --autostash, --any-branch remain later phases — do not implement their behaviour.)
- Test end-to-end with FakeRunner + RecordingPresenter: assert the generated body flows identically to all three sinks; gate-accept proceeds to Record; gate-abort leaves the repo clean; exercise the normal-AI, --no-ai, and degenerate paths; assert the recorded event sequence matches the protocol.

Acceptance Criteria:
- A prior-tag mint release generates the body via the precedence resolver and gates on review.
- On accept (or the -y auto-accepted default), the identical body is recorded, tagged, atomic-pushed, and published — same body in all three sinks.
- The engine always calls Prompt for the review gate; under -y the skip happens inside Prompt (auto-accept echo, returns the gate default) — the engine never branches around the call.
- The run emits the as-built event protocol: RunStarted -> StageStarted/StageSucceeded (engine-set Blocking + engine-measured Elapsed) -> ShowPlan -> ShowNotes -> Prompt(NotesReviewGate()) -> ... -> RunFinished(RunResult) on success; the gate-n abort path ends in Unwound.
- Gate accept proceeds to Record; gate abort (n) leaves the repo clean.
- The normal-AI, --no-ai, and degenerate paths each run end-to-end.
- last_tag..HEAD is the diff base for the AI path; first-release still resolves to 'Initial release.' via the same selector.
- The PONR asymmetry preserved (pre-push abort; post-push publish failure warns only).
- Whole flow exercised with FakeRunner + RecordingPresenter; no real git/gh/claude in tests.

Tests: a prior-tag release generates a body and flows it identically to all three sinks; gate accept proceeds to Record, tag, push, and publish; gate abort leaves the repo clean; the recorder Kinds() sequence matches the event protocol — RunStarted through ShowPlan/ShowNotes/Prompt to RunFinished on success, ending in Unwound on gate-abort; under -y Prompt still fires exactly once (KindPrompt recorded) and the run proceeds on the returned default; the --no-ai path runs end-to-end with a fallback body; a degenerate diff runs end-to-end with the stub body and no AI call; a publish failure after a successful push warns only (post-PONR).

Edge Cases: generated body flows to all three sinks; gate accept proceeds to record; gate abort leaves repo clean; -y auto-accepts inside Prompt (Prompt still called).

Context: Phase 2 goal — releases with a prior tag generate a notes body from the last_tag..HEAD diff via the layered AI engine, prepend a computed Change Map, distribute the single body whole to the tag annotation/CHANGELOG.md/provider release, and gate on the interactive y/n/e/r notes review. Stage 4: generate once, use everywhere. Release Lifecycle spine: Version -> Preflight -> (Project prep) -> Release notes -> Record -> Make official (tag + git push --atomic) -> Publish. The run reports through the as-built Presenter event protocol (RunStarted/stages/ShowPlan/ShowNotes/Prompt(NotesReviewGate())/RunFinished, or Unwound on abort). Invariants & Failure model (Phase 1, preserved): pre-push local-only and recoverable; git push --atomic is the single PONR; post-PONR publish failure warns only. Notes-path precedence unifies first-release / degenerate / --no-ai / normal AI under one selector. --dry-run/--set-version/--autostash/--any-branch are later phases.

Spec Reference: specification.md — Release Lifecycle (the spine); Stage 4 AI Release Notes; Body Distribution; Interactive Confirmation & Notes Review; Stages 6-7 Failure model.

title: Phase 3: Project Prep — Hooks, Version-File Projection & Diff Exclusion
Hook runner foundation (sh -c, repo root, MINT_* env, string|array)
description:
Problem: Hooks are mint's escape valve for project-specific steps. Configured as [release.hooks] table keyed by lifecycle point, each value a string or array of strings, run sequentially via sh -c from the repo root with injected MINT_* env vars. None of the three points (preflight/pre_tag/post_release) can run without a single shared mechanism that resolves a hook value to a sequence of commands, executes each via sh -c from the repo root with env injected, and reports the first non-zero exit.

Solution: A hook-runner taking a hook value (string or []string) and a context (resolved MINT_* env), normalising the value to a sequence of command entries, running each as sh -c '<entry>' through the Phase 1 CommandRunner from the repo root with env injected. Returns success when all exit zero, or the failing entry's result on first non-zero exit (stopping the sequence). Mechanism-only; the three points and their pre/post-PONR semantics are 3-2/3-3/3-4.

Solution note: builds the shared invocation mechanism and MINT_* env assembly only. Does NOT decide abort-vs-warn (per-point: 3-2/3-3 abort, 3-4 warns) and does NOT wire any specific point into the spine. MINT_DRY_RUN is part of the injected set, but the dry-run skip-and-report is 3-11.

Outcome: a single string -> one sh -c command; an array -> entries in order; from repo root; each with MINT_NEW_VERSION/MINT_PREVIOUS_VERSION/MINT_VERSION_TAG/MINT_BUMP/MINT_DRY_RUN injected. First non-zero exit stops the sequence and returns that entry's failure (later entries do not run). Empty/absent value -> no-op success. MINT_BUMP is patch/minor/major for bump flags and explicit when --set-version was used.

Do:
- internal/hooks (new): Run(ctx, value any, env HookEnv) error where value is the parsed TOML value (string or []string) and HookEnv carries injected vars.
- Normalise value to []string entries: string -> one entry; array -> entries in declared order; absent/empty (nil/empty string/empty array) -> no entries -> no-op success.
- Execute each entry via CommandRunner as sh -c '<entry>' (name sh, args [-c, entry]) so &&, pipes, env vars, ./script.sh work.
- Run from repo root — set working directory to resolved repo root (git rev-parse --show-toplevel, 1-4). Thread repo root through the CommandRunner working-directory affordance (do not chdir the process globally).
- Inject MINT_* env per entry layered on inherited env: MINT_NEW_VERSION (version being released), MINT_PREVIOUS_VERSION (prior latest), MINT_VERSION_TAG (full tag with prefix), MINT_BUMP (patch/minor/major/explicit; explicit when --set-version), MINT_DRY_RUN (0/1).
- Sequencing/first-failure: run in order; on first non-zero exit stop and return that entry's failure (failing command + stderr/exit). Remaining entries not run. Whether caller aborts or warns is decided by the point (3-2/3-3 abort, 3-4 warns).
- Assemble HookEnv from computed version state so each point reuses the same builder.
- All execution via CommandRunner; tests use FakeRunner to script per-entry exit codes and assert the sh -c argv, working directory, injected env, invocation order.

Acceptance Criteria:
- A single string hook runs as exactly one sh -c '<string>' command.
- An array hook runs each entry as sh -c '<entry>' in declared order.
- All entries run via CommandRunner with working directory set to repo root.
- Each entry invoked with MINT_NEW_VERSION/MINT_PREVIOUS_VERSION/MINT_VERSION_TAG/MINT_BUMP/MINT_DRY_RUN injected on top of inherited env.
- MINT_BUMP is patch/minor/major for bump flags and explicit when --set-version was used.
- First non-zero exit stops the sequence; later entries do not run; failing entry's result returned.
- Empty/absent hook value is a no-op.
- All command execution via CommandRunner/FakeRunner.

Tests: single string hook runs as one sh -c command; array hook runs each entry via sh -c in order; hooks run with working directory set to repo root; each entry invoked with MINT_* env injected; MINT_BUMP is explicit when --set-version used; first non-zero exit stops sequence and returns the failure; absent/empty hook value is a no-op.

Edge Cases: string single command vs array run in order; first non-zero exit stops sequence; MINT_BUMP=explicit for --set-version; empty/absent hook -> no-op.

Context: Mechanism — hooks are a config table of shell commands keyed by lifecycle point ([release.hooks]); value is a string or array; array entries run sequentially, first non-zero exit aborts (pre-PONR hooks); executed through sh -c so &&/pipes/env/./script.sh work; run from repo root. Invocation & context: each entry runs via sh -c from repo root; mint injects MINT_NEW_VERSION, MINT_PREVIOUS_VERSION, MINT_VERSION_TAG, MINT_BUMP (patch/minor/major/explicit, explicit when --set-version), MINT_DRY_RUN (0/1); set may grow. Hooks nest under [release.hooks]. Phase 3 reads only needed config keys with defaults; full schema validation Phase 6.

Spec Reference: specification.md — Hooks Mechanism / Invocation & context (injected env vars).

title: preflight hook (runs after built-in gates, aborts on non-zero)
description:
Problem: A project may need its own gates/validation beyond mint's built-in preflight checks. The optional preflight hook must run AFTER mint's built-in preflight checks pass (Stage 2) and BEFORE any mutation, so project-specific validation gates the release in the same pre-mutation, fully-recoverable window. A non-zero exit must abort the whole release cleanly (nothing tagged, no damage).

Solution: Wire the preflight hook into the spine immediately after the built-in preflight gates (1-5/1-6) and before any mutation (before Stage 3 prep / Stage 5 record). Resolve [release.hooks].preflight and run it via the hook runner (3-1) with MINT_* env. A non-zero exit aborts cleanly via the Presenter; an absent hook is skipped silently.

Solution note: wires the preflight point only. Runner mechanism, env assembly, string/array handling, first-failure sequencing already exist (3-1). pre_tag (3-3) and post_release (3-4) are separate points. Because preflight runs before any mutation, a clean abort here has nothing to unwind; the surgical auto-unwind hardening is Phase 4 and not exercised by this point.

Outcome: after the built-in gates pass, mint runs the preflight hook (string or array) from repo root with MINT_* injected. Zero exit -> proceed to project prep/notes. Non-zero exit (or first non-zero array entry) -> abort cleanly (no mutation occurred, repo untouched), surfaced via the Presenter naming the failing hook. Absent preflight hook -> skipped.

Do:
- In the orchestrator (extending the Phase 1 spine), invoke the preflight hook AFTER the built-in preflight gates pass (1-5 local + 1-6 network) and BEFORE any mutation (before the pre_tag hook / Record).
- Read [release.hooks].preflight (string/array/absent); pass to the hook runner (3-1) with assembled MINT_* env.
- Absent hook -> skip (no-op): release proceeds as if no hook configured.
- Non-zero exit -> clean abort: stop the release before any mutation, surface via the Presenter (e.g. 'preflight hook failed: <command> (exit N)'), exit non-zero. For an array, the first non-zero entry aborts (3-1 first-failure rule).
- Confirm ordering against the spine: built-in gates -> preflight hook -> (later) pre_tag prep -> notes -> record. Never runs before the built-in gates and never after a mutation.
- Test via FakeRunner + RecordingPresenter: hook runs only after built-in gates pass; a non-zero exit aborts before any commit/tag; an absent hook is skipped.

Acceptance Criteria:
- preflight hook runs after the built-in preflight gates pass and before any mutation.
- Zero exit lets the release proceed.
- Non-zero exit aborts cleanly (no mutation, repo untouched) and surfaces the failing hook via the Presenter.
- For an array, the first non-zero entry aborts (no later entries run).
- Absent preflight hook skipped silently.
- Hook runs from repo root with MINT_* env injected (via 3-1 runner).
- All execution via CommandRunner/FakeRunner; Presenter records the abort.

Tests: preflight hook runs after built-in gates pass; non-zero preflight hook aborts cleanly before any mutation; array preflight hook aborts on first non-zero entry; absent preflight hook skipped; preflight hook does not run if a built-in gate fails first.

Edge Cases: runs after built-in gates pass; non-zero -> clean abort pre-mutation; absent hook -> skipped; array first failure aborts.

Context: Project preflight hook — after mint's built-in preflight checks pass, the project's optional preflight hook runs (project-specific gates/validation), before any mutation; a non-zero exit aborts the release cleanly. Hook points: preflight runs after mint's built-in preflight checks (Stage 2), before any mutation. Failure behaviour: preflight/pre_tag run before the tag is pushed -> a non-zero exit aborts the whole release cleanly (no tag, no damage; mint auto-unwinds any local mutations). Because preflight precedes all mutation, the abort has nothing to unwind; surgical auto-unwind is Phase 4.

Spec Reference: specification.md — Stage 2 Project preflight hook; Hooks Hook points / Failure behaviour.

title: pre_tag hook execution & artifact commit (commit-interplay rule)
description:
Problem: Projects often need to build/generate artifacts (e.g. a knowledge bundle) before tagging. The pre_tag hook runs at Stage 3 project prep and may dirty the tree; mint must then commit whatever the hook left dirty as its OWN commit (chore(release): pre-tag artifacts for {tag}), kept separate from bookkeeping because it is project content. Subtle cases: clean tree -> no commit; hook that makes its own commit and hands back clean -> mint commits nothing; gitignored outputs don't count as dirty. Non-zero exit aborts cleanly.

Solution: Wire the pre_tag hook into the spine at Stage 3 (after preflight, before notes). Run via the hook runner (3-1). After success, check whether the tree is dirty (tracked changes and/or non-ignored untracked files, per Phase 1 porcelain convention); if dirty, stage and commit as chore(release): pre-tag artifacts for {tag} — distinct from the bookkeeping commit. If clean (built nothing, or made its own commit), commit nothing. A non-zero hook exit aborts cleanly.

Solution note: lands the pre_tag point and its artifact commit (commit graph step 1). The bookkeeping commit (changelog + version-file fold) is step 2 (3-5/3-6/3-7). Two-commit assembly and tag-points-at-bookkeeping ordering is 3-8. Notes generate at post-hook HEAD (Phase 2 diff base last_tag..HEAD); diff_exclude filtering of hook artifacts is 3-9/3-10.

Outcome: hook dirties tree -> exactly one own commit chore(release): pre-tag artifacts for {tag} capturing dirtied tracked + non-ignored-untracked changes. Clean tree (built nothing, or made own commit + handed back clean) -> no pre-tag commit. Gitignored build outputs don't count. Non-zero hook exit aborts cleanly before any tag/push.

Do:
- Invoke the pre_tag hook at Stage 3 — after preflight (3-2), before notes (Stage 4). Read [release.hooks].pre_tag (string/array/absent) and run via 3-1 with MINT_* env.
- Absent hook -> skip (no prep, no commit).
- Non-zero exit -> clean abort: stop before any tag/push, surface via the Presenter. (Array: first non-zero entry aborts — 3-1.) On a hook failure mint has not yet committed.
- After a successful hook, evaluate tree dirtiness using Phase 1 clean-tree convention (git status --porcelain): tracked changes and/or non-ignored untracked files count as dirty; gitignored exempt.
  - Dirty -> commit: git add the dirtied paths and git commit subject chore(release): pre-tag artifacts for {tag} via CommandRunner. Its OWN commit, semantically distinct from the bookkeeping commit.
  - Clean -> no commit: built nothing, or made its own commit + handed back clean -> mint commits nothing (commit-only-if-changed falls out for free; custom-commit hook respected).
- Keep this commit separate from the bookkeeping commit ({commit_prefix} Release {tag}); do not fold.
- Test via FakeRunner + RecordingPresenter: hook dirties tree -> one chore(release) commit; clean tree -> no commit; hook makes own commit + clean -> no mint commit; gitignored-only -> no commit; non-zero hook -> clean abort.

Acceptance Criteria:
- pre_tag hook runs at Stage 3, after preflight and before notes generation.
- Hook dirties tree -> one commit chore(release): pre-tag artifacts for {tag} (own commit, separate from bookkeeping).
- Clean tree after hook -> no pre-tag commit.
- Hook makes its own commit and hands back clean -> mint commits nothing.
- Gitignored outputs do not count as dirty.
- Non-zero hook exit aborts cleanly before any tag/push.
- Artifact commit distinct from (not folded into) the bookkeeping commit.
- All git operations via CommandRunner/FakeRunner.

Tests: pre_tag hook that dirties the tree produces its own chore(release) artifact commit; clean tree after hook produces no pre-tag commit; hook that makes its own commit and leaves a clean tree produces no mint commit; gitignored outputs do not count as dirty; non-zero pre_tag hook aborts cleanly; artifact commit separate from the bookkeeping commit.

Edge Cases: hook dirties tree -> own commit; clean tree -> no commit; hook makes own commit + hands back clean -> nothing committed; non-zero exit -> clean abort; gitignored outputs don't count as dirty.

Context: Hook points — pre_tag is Stage 3 project prep (build/generate artifacts, e.g. a knowledge bundle); dirties the tree -> mint commits per the interplay rule. Commit interplay: after a pre_tag hook runs, mint commits whatever it left dirty (chore(release): pre-tag artifacts for {tag}); simple hooks never touch git; commit-only-if-changed falls out for free; a hook that wants a custom commit can do its own and hand back a clean tree; mint never tags a dirty tree. Commit graph: hook artifacts (only if a pre_tag hook dirtied the tree) -> their own commit, kept separate (project content, distinct from release bookkeeping). Clean-tree convention (Phase 1): git status --porcelain; gitignored exempt; non-ignored untracked count. Notes generate at the post-hook HEAD.

Spec Reference: specification.md — Hooks Hook points / Commit interplay (pre_tag); Stage 5 Commit graph.

title: post_release hook (warn-only on failure)
description:
Problem: After the provider release is created (Stage 7), a project may need follow-ups (notifications, tap repository_dispatch, etc.). The post_release hook runs after publish, PAST the point of no return, so unlike pre-PONR hooks it CANNOT abort — a non-zero exit must WARN only ('post_release hook failed; tag is already published'), same principle as a failed gh release create.

Solution: Wire the post_release hook into the spine at Stage 7, after the provider release is created. Run via the hook runner (3-1) with MINT_* env. On any non-zero exit, warn via the Presenter and continue — never abort, never unwind (the tag is already public). Absent hook -> skipped.

Solution note: lands the post_release point and its warn-only semantics. The publish step (Stage 7 Publisher.CreateRelease) already exists (Phase 1, 1-8/1-11). post_release runs after publish. Array nuance: post-PONR, the runner's first-non-zero-stops-the-sequence rule still stops the array, but the outcome is a warning, not an abort.

Outcome: after a successful publish, mint runs the post_release hook (string or array) from repo root with MINT_* injected. Zero exit -> run completes normally. Non-zero exit (or first non-zero array entry) -> a warning via the Presenter ('post_release hook failed; tag is already published'), run still completes successfully (release already live; nothing unwound). Absent hook -> skipped.

Do:
- Invoke the post_release hook at Stage 7, AFTER the provider release create (1-8 Publisher.CreateRelease), i.e. after the point of no return. Read [release.hooks].post_release (string/array/absent) and run via 3-1 with MINT_* env.
- Absent hook -> skip.
- Non-zero exit -> warn only: surface a Presenter warning ('post_release hook failed; tag is already published'), do not abort, do not unwind. Run ends successfully. Mirrors warn-only of a failed gh release create after a successful push.
- Array semantics post-PONR: runner stops at the first non-zero entry (3-1); mint reports that failure as a warning rather than aborting. Document that stop-on-first-failure sequencing is the same; only the consequence (warn vs abort) differs by point.
- Ensure post_release is the only hook point whose failure is non-fatal; preflight/pre_tag (3-2/3-3) abort.
- Test via FakeRunner + RecordingPresenter: non-zero post_release hook -> a recorded warning and a successful run; hook runs after publish; absent hook skipped.

Acceptance Criteria:
- post_release hook runs at Stage 7, after the provider release is created.
- Zero exit completes the run normally.
- Non-zero exit warns ('post_release hook failed; tag is already published') and does NOT abort or unwind; run still completes.
- For an array, the first non-zero entry stops the sequence and is reported as a warning (not an abort).
- Absent post_release hook skipped.
- Hook runs from repo root with MINT_* env injected.
- All execution via CommandRunner/FakeRunner; warning recorded via the Presenter.

Tests: post_release hook runs after the provider release is created; non-zero post_release hook warns only and the run still completes; non-zero post_release hook does not unwind the published release; array post_release hook stops at the first failure and warns; absent post_release hook skipped.

Edge Cases: non-zero -> warn only; runs after publish; absent -> skipped; array continues semantics post-PONR (stop-on-first-failure, but warn not abort).

Context: Hook points — post_release is Stage 7 follow-ups after the provider release (notifications, tap repository_dispatch). Failure behaviour: post_release runs after the tag is live -> cannot abort; a non-zero exit just warns ('post_release hook failed; tag is already published'); same principle as a failed gh release create. Failure model: post_release hook fails -> warn only — after the point of no return, the tag is already published. Post-release tap/formula update is itself a post_release hook (repository_dispatch), already supported.

Spec Reference: specification.md — Hooks Hook points / Failure behaviour; Stages 6-7 Failure model / Post-release tap/formula update.

title: Version-file projection — plain mode (whole file is the version)
description:
Problem: Some projects need the new version written into the repo as a write-only mirror (e.g. release.txt whose whole contents are the version). In plain mode (version_file set, no version_pattern), mint writes the new version as the entire file contents during Record (Stage 5). The file is a derived mirror, never a source of truth. Must create the file if absent, and be a no-op when the file already holds the target version (no empty commit).

Solution: A plain-mode version-file writer in Record: when version_file is configured and version_pattern is NOT, write the new version as the whole file contents, creating the file if absent. If the file already holds exactly the target version, do nothing (so the bookkeeping commit can no-op). Embedded mode is 3-6; folding into the bookkeeping commit is 3-7.

Solution note: implements plain mode only. Embedded mode is 3-6. The version-file change folds into the single bookkeeping commit with the changelog (3-7) — this task produces the file write + no-op detection, not the commit. Decide and document whether the projected value carries tag_prefix — default to bare semver X.Y.Z (the version mirror, not the tag); note the choice so embedded mode (3-6) and tests align.

Outcome: with version_file set and no version_pattern, Record writes the new version as the whole contents (creating if absent). When the file already contains exactly the target version, the write is a no-op (no net change -> no empty commit downstream). The file is strictly a write-only mirror — mint never reads it as a version source (tag is truth, Stage 1).

Do:
- internal/record (extending Phase 1 Record): plain-mode projection — when [release].version_file set and [release].version_pattern absent, write the new version as the entire file contents.
- Create if absent: if version_file does not exist, create it at the configured path relative to the repo root.
- Value written: the new version string (bare X.Y.Z by default — document the decision). Decide trailing-newline handling explicitly and consistently (recommend a single trailing newline; document and test).
- No-op detection: if the file already holds exactly the target contents (same version, same newline convention), make no change so the downstream bookkeeping commit (3-7) sees nothing to stage for the version file.
- Do not create the commit here — staged and folded into the single bookkeeping commit in 3-7.
- The file is a write-only mirror; code note that it is never read as a version source (Stage 1: tag-as-truth).
- Test the writer directly: file absent -> created with target version; file holding old version -> overwritten; file already at target -> no change (no-op); trailing-newline handling consistent.

Acceptance Criteria:
- With version_file set and no version_pattern, the whole file is written as the new version.
- An absent version_file is created with the target version.
- A file holding an older version is overwritten with the new version.
- A file already holding exactly the target version is a no-op (no net change).
- Trailing-newline handling explicit and consistent (documented + tested).
- The version file treated as a write-only mirror (never read as a version source).
- The commit itself NOT created here (folded into the bookkeeping commit, 3-7).

Tests: plain mode writes the whole file as the new version; absent version_file created with the target version; file holding an old version overwritten; file already at target version is a no-op; trailing newline handling consistent.

Edge Cases: file absent -> created; file already holds target version -> no-op (no empty commit); trailing-newline handling.

Context: Optional version-file projection — when a project needs the version written into the repo, mint mirrors the new version into a file during Record (Stage 5); always a write-only mirror kept in sync, never a source of truth. version_file path to write; omit = tag-only. version_pattern omit = the whole file is the version (plain mode). Legacy mapping: old file (plain release.txt) -> version_file = release.txt, no pattern. Stage 5: when version_file configured, mint writes the new version (per version_pattern, or whole file in plain mode). No-op safety: no empty commits if the version file already holds the target version. Source of truth (Stage 1): version file is derived state, tag is truth. Bookkeeping commit folds changelog + version-file (3-7).

Spec Reference: specification.md — Stage 1 Optional version-file projection / Legacy strategy mapping; Stage 5 Version-file projection / No-op safety.

title: Version-file projection — embedded mode (version_pattern)
description:
Problem: Other projects embed the version inside a real source file (e.g. RELEASE_VERSION="1.4.0" in main.go). In embedded mode (version_file + version_pattern), mint substitutes the new version by replacing the configured pattern. Critically: a pattern matching NOTHING must abort during Record, before the tag (fail-loud, never silently skip), and a pattern matching MULTIPLE times must replace ALL occurrences (legacy sed-replace semantics) to keep the file internally consistent.

Solution: An embedded-mode version-file writer in Record: when version_file and version_pattern are both set, expand the pattern's {version} placeholder to derive a match expression, find all occurrences, and replace each with the new-version rendering. Zero matches -> abort the release before the tag (fail-loud, same family as a notes failure). Multiple matches -> replace all. No change needed (file at target) -> no-op. Folding into the bookkeeping commit is 3-7.

Solution note: implements embedded mode only. Plain mode is 3-5. The version-file change folds into the single bookkeeping commit (3-7). The {version} placeholder in version_pattern is the configured marker (e.g. RELEASE_VERSION="{version}"); mint matches the pattern treating the placeholder as the version slot and writes the new version into it. Align the {version} rendering (bare X.Y.Z) with 3-5.

Outcome: with version_file + version_pattern, Record replaces every occurrence of the pattern (old version slot) with the new version. A pattern matching nothing aborts the release before the tag, fail-loud, clear message (the version write is never silently skipped). Multiple matches all replaced (no stale copies). A file already at the target (pattern present, value already the new version) is a no-op. Bookkeeping commit (3-7) folds the change in.

Do:
- internal/record: embedded-mode projection — when [release].version_file and [release].version_pattern both set, perform pattern substitution.
- Pattern semantics: version_pattern contains a {version} placeholder (e.g. RELEASE_VERSION="{version}"). Build a matcher where {version} stands for the version slot (matching any current version value), and a replacement where {version} is the new version. Treat non-{version} parts of the pattern as literal text anchoring the match. Render {version} as bare X.Y.Z (consistent with 3-5).
- Mismatch (zero matches) -> abort before the tag: if the pattern matches nothing, fail loud during Record — abort before any tag is created, clear message (e.g. 'version_pattern matched nothing in <file>'). Never silently skip. Same fail-loud family as a notes failure (Record precedes the tag).
- Multiple matches -> replace all: replace every occurrence of the version slot (legacy sed-replace), file internally consistent, no stale old-version copies.
- No-op: if the file already contains the pattern with the new version in every slot (substitution yields identical contents), make no change (contributes to no-op safety).
- Do not create the commit here — staged and folded into the single bookkeeping commit (3-7).
- Test the substitution directly: single match replaced; multiple matches all replaced; zero matches -> abort-before-tag error; already-at-target -> no-op; {version} placeholder correctly substituted into the replacement.

Acceptance Criteria:
- With version_file + version_pattern, the pattern's version slot is replaced with the new version.
- A pattern matching nothing aborts the release before the tag (fail-loud), never silently skipping.
- Multiple matches all replaced (no stale old-version copies remain).
- A file already at the target version (every slot already the new version) is a no-op.
- The {version} placeholder correctly substituted in both the match and the replacement.
- The commit itself NOT created here (folded into the bookkeeping commit, 3-7).

Tests: embedded mode replaces the version_pattern slot with the new version; a version_pattern matching nothing aborts before the tag; multiple matches all replaced; a file already at the target version is a no-op; the {version} placeholder substituted in the replacement.

Edge Cases: pattern matches nothing -> abort before tag; multiple matches -> all replaced; already at target version -> no-op; {version} placeholder substitution.

Context: Optional version-file projection — version_pattern e.g. RELEASE_VERSION="{version}"; omit = whole file is the version (plain mode). Legacy mapping: old embedded (sed-replace into a source file) -> version_file + version_pattern = RELEASE_VERSION="{version}". Stage 5: version_pattern mismatch (matches nothing) -> abort during Record, before the tag (fail-loud, same family as a notes failure); never silently skip the version write. version_pattern multiple matches -> mint replaces all occurrences (legacy sed-replace), keeping the file internally consistent rather than leaving stale copies. The change folds into the single bookkeeping commit (3-7); no-op when already at target.

Spec Reference: specification.md — Stage 1 Optional version-file projection / Legacy strategy mapping; Stage 5 Version-file projection.

title: Bookkeeping commit folds changelog + version-file projection
description:
Problem: The legacy script made three commits per release — needlessly noisy. mint folds the CHANGELOG entry AND the version-file projection into ONE release-bookkeeping commit ({commit_prefix} Release {tag}). Phase 1 built this commit for the changelog alone; now the version-file projection (3-5/3-6) must be staged into the SAME commit, with no-op safety across both inputs: neither nets a change -> no commit; either changes -> exactly one commit.

Solution: Extend the Phase 1 bookkeeping-commit step so that, before committing, it runs the version-file projection (plain 3-5 or embedded 3-6 per config) AND writes the changelog (Phase 1), stages both, and creates the single {commit_prefix} Release {tag} commit. No-op safety spans both: commit only if at least one produced a net change; otherwise skip.

Solution note: folds the version-file write into the existing bookkeeping commit. Depends on 3-5/3-6 for the file writes and Phase 1 (1-9) for changelog write + commit mechanics. The embedded-mode pattern-mismatch abort (3-6) fires BEFORE this commit (during the version-file write). The separate hook-artifact commit (3-3) is distinct and precedes this commit in the graph (3-8).

Outcome: both changelog and version file change -> one commit {commit_prefix} Release {tag} containing both. Version file unchanged but changelog changes -> still the one commit (just the changelog). Nothing nets a change (changelog no-op and version file at target, or no version_file and no-op changelog) -> no commit (no empty commit). The version file is never given its own separate commit.

Do:
- Extend the Phase 1 Record step (1-9 bookkeeping commit) so, in order:
  1. Write the changelog entry (Phase 1 writer; skipped when changelog=false).
  2. Run the version-file projection if version_file configured: plain (3-5) or embedded (3-6) per whether version_pattern is set. (Embedded mismatch aborts here, before any commit — 3-6.)
  3. Stage both the changelog change and the version-file change.
  4. Create ONE commit {commit_prefix} Release {tag} containing both.
- No-op safety across both inputs: commit only if at least one of {changelog, version file} produced a net change. If both no-ops (changelog no net change AND version file at target, or no version_file), make no commit. Reuse Phase 1 no-op-skip extended to consider the version-file change too.
- Do not give the version file its own commit — always folded.
- Keep this commit distinct from the pre_tag hook-artifact commit (3-3): that one precedes; this is release bookkeeping.
- Test via FakeRunner: both change -> one commit staging both; version unchanged but changelog changes -> still one commit; nothing net-changed -> no commit; version_file absent -> behaves as Phase 1 (changelog-only bookkeeping commit).

Acceptance Criteria:
- Both changelog and version file change -> exactly one {commit_prefix} Release {tag} commit containing both.
- Version file unchanged but changelog changes -> one commit still made.
- Neither nets a change -> no commit (no empty commit).
- The version file never given its own separate commit (always folded in).
- With no version_file configured, behaviour matches Phase 1 (changelog-only bookkeeping commit).
- The bookkeeping commit distinct from the pre_tag hook-artifact commit.
- All git operations via CommandRunner/FakeRunner.

Tests: changelog + version file change fold into one bookkeeping commit; version unchanged but changelog changed still commits once; nothing net-changed makes no commit (no empty commit); the version file is never given its own commit; with no version_file behaviour matches the Phase 1 bookkeeping commit.

Edge Cases: both changelog and version file change -> one commit; version unchanged but changelog changes -> still commits; nothing net-changed -> no empty commit.

Context: Commit graph — release bookkeeping: the CHANGELOG entry and the version-file projection folded into one commit {commit_prefix} Release {tag} (subject uses the configurable commit_prefix, default emoji); the legacy script made three commits per release. No-op safety: no empty commits if the changelog yields no net change, or the version file already holds the target version. Hook artifacts get their own separate commit (3-3); the bookkeeping commit folds changelog + version-file. Phase 1 (1-9) built the changelog-only bookkeeping commit with no-op skip; this extends it to fold the version file.

Spec Reference: specification.md — Stage 5 Commit graph / No-op safety.

title: Up-to-two-commit graph (hook-artifact then bookkeeping)
description:
Problem: A release now produces up to two commits before the tag: an optional pre_tag hook-artifact commit (Stage 3, 3-3) then the release-bookkeeping commit (Stage 5, 3-7). The tag must point at the BOOKKEEPING commit, and no-op safety must hold throughout (no empty commits). The combinations — both, only bookkeeping, only hook, or neither — must all resolve correctly, and the tag must always sit at HEAD after Record.

Solution: Assemble the commit graph in the spine: Stage 3 may produce the hook-artifact commit (3-3), Stage 5 may produce the bookkeeping commit (3-7); the annotated tag (Phase 1, 1-10) is then created at the resulting HEAD (the bookkeeping commit when one was made, else the last commit present). The atomic push (Phase 1) sends both commits + tag together. No-op safety from 3-3/3-7 means zero, one, or two commits can result; the tag always points at the bookkeeping commit / HEAD.

Solution note: wires the ordering and the tag target; the individual commits are 3-3 (hook artifact) and 3-7 (bookkeeping). The atomic push and PONR are Phase 1 (1-10). The neither-dirty -> zero-commits case means the bookkeeping commit no-ops too; the tag then points at the pre-existing HEAD; whether that is a desirable release is the degenerate/no-op path handled upstream (Phase 2), not re-decided here.

Outcome: dirtying pre_tag hook + changed changelog/version -> two commits (hook-artifact then bookkeeping), tag at the bookkeeping commit. No hook dirt but a changelog/version change -> one (bookkeeping) commit, tag at it. Hook dirt but no bookkeeping change -> one (hook-artifact) commit, tag at HEAD. Neither nets a change -> zero new commits, tag at existing HEAD. In every case the tag points at the bookkeeping commit when one exists, else at HEAD; atomic push sends commits + tag together.

Do:
- Assemble the commit graph in spine order:
  1. Stage 3: run the pre_tag hook and, if it dirtied the tree, create the hook-artifact commit chore(release): pre-tag artifacts for {tag} (3-3). Zero or one commit.
  2. Stage 5 (Record): write changelog + version-file projection and, if either netted a change, create the bookkeeping commit {commit_prefix} Release {tag} (3-7). Zero or one commit.
  3. Tag: create the annotated tag (1-10) at the bookkeeping commit when one was made; if the bookkeeping commit no-opped, the tag points at the current HEAD (the hook-artifact commit, or the pre-existing HEAD if neither commit was made).
  4. Push: git push --atomic origin HEAD {tag} (Phase 1) sends the up-to-two commits + tag together.
- Ensure the tag target is always correct: the bookkeeping commit when present, else HEAD. Document that the tag conceptually points at the release-bookkeeping commit and HEAD equals that commit when one was made.
- Preserve no-op safety end to end: zero, one, or two commits all valid; never create an empty commit at either step (3-3/3-7 guard this — do not reintroduce empties when combining).
- Test via FakeRunner: hook dirt + bookkeeping change -> two commits, tag at bookkeeping; no hook dirt + bookkeeping change -> one commit, tag at it; hook dirt + no bookkeeping change -> one (hook-artifact) commit, tag at HEAD; neither -> zero commits, tag at existing HEAD; atomic push includes all commits + the tag.

Acceptance Criteria:
- A dirtying hook + a changelog/version change -> two commits (hook-artifact then bookkeeping).
- No hook dirt + a changelog/version change -> one (bookkeeping) commit.
- Hook dirt + no bookkeeping change -> one (hook-artifact) commit; tag points at HEAD.
- Neither dirty -> zero new commits; tag points at the existing HEAD.
- The tag points at the bookkeeping commit when one exists, else at HEAD.
- No empty commit ever created when combining the two steps.
- The atomic push sends all resulting commits + the tag together.
- All git operations via CommandRunner/FakeRunner.

Tests: a dirtying hook plus a bookkeeping change produces two commits with the tag at the bookkeeping commit; no hook dirt plus a bookkeeping change produces one commit; hook dirt with no bookkeeping change produces one hook-artifact commit and the tag at HEAD; neither dirty produces zero commits and the tag at the existing HEAD; the atomic push includes all resulting commits and the tag.

Edge Cases: hook commit + bookkeeping commit (two); no hook dirt -> one commit; neither dirty -> zero commits; tag always points at bookkeeping/HEAD.

Context: Commit graph (up to two commits, then tag): 1 hook artifacts (only if a pre_tag hook dirtied the tree) -> their own commit chore(release): pre-tag artifacts for {tag}; 2 release bookkeeping — CHANGELOG entry and version-file projection folded into one commit {commit_prefix} Release {tag}; 3 annotated tag points at the release-bookkeeping commit; 4 git push --atomic sends both commits + tag together — the single point of no return. No-op safety: no empty commits. Phase 1 built the tag + atomic push (1-10); 3-3/3-7 build the individual commits.

Spec Reference: specification.md — Stage 5 Commit graph / No-op safety; Stages 6-7 Point of no return.

title: Configurable diff_exclude globs (on top of built-in CHANGELOG.md)
description:
Problem: Projects carry tracked, committed generated files (knowledge bundles, minified output, lockfiles, generated code) deliberately not in .gitignore but which should not pollute the AI's view of the release. The diff_exclude glob array must apply via git :(exclude) ON TOP OF the built-in CHANGELOG.md exclusion (Phase 2), and excluded paths must not count toward max_diff_lines.

Solution: Extend the Phase 2 diff context assembly (2-2) and Change Map (2-4) so the assembled git pathspec includes one :(exclude)<glob> entry per diff_exclude glob, IN ADDITION TO the always-excluded CHANGELOG.md. Because git does the filtering and the max_diff_lines count (2-3) runs on the already-excluded diff, excluded paths inherently don't count. Exclusion remains path-based, never commit-based.

Solution note: adds the diff_exclude tier to the existing exclusion machinery from Phase 2 — does not rebuild diff assembly. The strategy-aware version_file exclusion is the separate 3-10. diff_exclude was already read into config in Phase 2; this task applies it to the pathspec. Globs are passed to git as pathspec exclude patterns — let git interpret them (don't reimplement glob matching in Go).

Outcome: with diff_exclude = [skills/**/knowledge.cjs, *.min.js] (example), the assembled diff and Change Map exclude every matching path AND CHANGELOG.md. Multiple globs all apply. A glob matching nothing is harmless. A force-added gitignored (tracked) file matching a glob is still excluded (exclusion is by path; a tracked file can appear in the diff — the glob removes it). Excluded-path lines never count toward max_diff_lines (count runs post-exclusion).

Do:
- Extend the Phase 2 pathspec construction (2-2 AssembleDiff and 2-4 Change Map git commands) to append one ':(exclude)<glob>' pathspec entry for EACH diff_exclude glob, alongside the built-in ':(exclude)CHANGELOG.md'.
- Read the top-level shared diff_exclude key as an array of globs; absent -> empty array (only CHANGELOG.md excluded). Full schema validation Phase 6; Phase 3 reads with a default.
- Let git interpret the globs as pathspec patterns (:(exclude) semantics) — do not reimplement glob matching in Go. Multiple globs -> multiple :(exclude) entries.
- Confirm path-based, never commit-based exclusion (consistent with Phase 2): globs remove matching paths regardless of which commit introduced them.
- Force-added gitignored files: gitignored-yet-force-added is tracked and can appear in the diff; a diff_exclude glob matching it still excludes it. Intended interaction, not special-cased beyond the glob applying.
- max_diff_lines interaction: because the count (2-3) runs on the post-exclusion diff, the newly-excluded diff_exclude paths inherently don't count — verify (no separate code beyond ordering exclusion before counting).
- Combine cleanly with the built-in CHANGELOG.md exclusion: both tiers apply together.
- Test via FakeRunner: assert the assembled git pathspec carries :(exclude)CHANGELOG.md plus one :(exclude)<glob> per configured glob; multiple globs; a non-matching glob harmless; a force-added tracked file matching a glob excluded; excluded paths don't count toward max_diff_lines.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Each diff_exclude glob becomes a :(exclude)<glob> pathspec entry, in addition to :(exclude)CHANGELOG.md.
- Multiple globs all apply.
- A glob matching nothing has no effect (harmless).
- A force-added gitignored (tracked) file matching a glob is still excluded.
- Excluded paths do not count toward max_diff_lines (count runs post-exclusion).
- Exclusion path-based, never commit-based.
- diff_exclude absent -> only CHANGELOG.md excluded.
- Git performs the glob matching (not reimplemented in Go); all calls via CommandRunner/FakeRunner.

Tests: each diff_exclude glob applied as a git exclude pathspec on top of CHANGELOG.md; multiple diff_exclude globs all apply; a diff_exclude glob matching nothing is harmless; a force-added gitignored tracked file excluded by a matching glob; diff_exclude-excluded paths do not count toward max_diff_lines; absent diff_exclude excludes only CHANGELOG.md.

Edge Cases: multiple globs; glob matches nothing; force-added gitignored file still excluded by glob; excluded paths not counted toward max_diff_lines; combined with CHANGELOG.md exclusion.
